2004-06-09‚Äé"Which Historical Lunatic Are You? You are Nicola Tesla
column2: inventor of the Tesla Coil!   A minister's son from Simljan in Austria-Hungary
column3: you were precocious from an early age. At three you could multiply three-digit numbers in your head and calculate how many seconds visitors to your home had lived. In awe of your older brother Dane
column4: you shot a pea-shooter at his horse
column5: causing it to throw him and inflict injuries from which he later died. This tragedy haunted you ever after. You frequently suffered bouts of illness with hallucinations throughout your life. During one affliction of cholera
column6: you encountered the writing of Mark Twain
column7: with whom you were later to be close friends. Later
column10: illness inexplicably heightened your senses to a painful extent
column11: only relenting when you hit upon the idea of the alternating current motor.   You developed an aversion to human contact
column13: and a fear of pearls; when one would-be lover kissed you
column14: you ran away in agony. Later
column15: you insisted that any repeated actions in your day-to-day life had to be divisible by three
column20: continue walking until you had executed the required number of footsteps. You refused to eat anything until you had calculated its exact volume. Saltine crackers were a favourite for their uniformity in this respect. In the midst of important work
column21: you forgot trivial details such as eating
column26: began on a suitably bizarre note. The first was a frog-catching device that was so successful
column27: and hence so emulated by your fellow children
column28: that local frogs were almost eradicated. You also created a turbine powered by gluing sixteen May bugs to a tiny windmill. The insects panicked and flapped their wings furiously
column29: powering the contraption for hours on end. This worked admirably until a small child came along and ate all the creatures alive
column30: after which you never again touched another insect.   Prompted by dreams of attaining the then-ridiculed goal of achieving an alternating-current motor
column31: you went to America in the hope of teaming up with Thomas Edison. Edison snubbed you
column32: but promised fifty thousand dollars if you could improve his own direct-current motor by 20% efficiency. You succeeded. Edison did not pay up. It was not long until you created an AC motor by yourself.   Now successful
column33: you set up a small laboratory
column34: with a few assistants and almost no written records whatsoever. Despite it being destroyed by fire
column36: impressing even the least astute observer with man-made lightning and lights lit seemingly by magic. Moving to Colorado Springs
column37: you created a machine capable of sending ten million volts into the Earth's surface
column38: which even while being started up caused lightning to shoot from fire hydrants and sparks to singe feet through shoes all over the town. When calibrated to be in tune with the planet's resonance
column39: it created what is still the largest man-made electrical surge ever
column40: an arc over 130 feet long. Unfortunately
column41: it set the local power plant aflame.   You returned to New York
column42: incidentally toying with the nascent idea of something eerily like today's internet. Although the wealthiest man in America withdrew funding for a larger
column43: more powerful resonator in short order
column44: it did not stop you announcing the ability to split the world in two. You grew ever more diverse in your inventions: remote-controlled boats and submarines
column48: a death ray.   While whether the ray ever existed is still doubtful
column49: it is said that you notified the Peary polar expedition to report anything strange in the tundra
column50: and turned on the ray. First
column51: nothing happened; then it disintegrated an owl; finally
column52: reports reached you of the mysterious Tunguska explosion
column53: upon which news you dismantled the apparatus immediately. An offer during WWII to recreate it was
column55: never acted upon by then-President Wilson. Turning to other matters
column56: you investigated the forerunner of radar
column57: to widespread derision.   Your inventions grew stranger. One oscillator caused earthquakes in Manhattan. You adapted this for medical purposes
column58: claiming various health benefits for your devices. You found they let you work for days without sleep; Mark Twain enjoyed the experience until the sudden onset of diarrhoea. You claimed to receive signals in quasi-Morse Code from Mars
column59: explored the initial stages of quantum physics; proposed a ""wall of light""
column62: anti-gravity airships and time travel; and proposed a basic design for a machine for photographing thoughts. You died aged 87 in New York
column63: sharing an apartment with the flock of pigeons who were by then your only friends.   Ridiculed throughout your life (Superman fought the evil Dr. Tesla in 1940s comics)
column64: you were posthumously declared the father of the fluorescent bulb
column65: the vacuum tube amplifier and the X-ray machine
column66: and the Supreme Court named you as the legal inventor of the radio in place of Marconi. Wardenclyffe
column67: the tower once housing your death ray
column68: was dynamited several times to stop it falling into the hands of spies. It was strangely hard to topple
column69: and even then could not be broken up.      urlLink     urlLink Which Historical Lunatic Are You?    urlLink From the fecund loins of Rum and Monkey."
